content.full_text:
Farn Polrrlcal PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
Iune 14,2004
Ronald R. Ball, City Attomey
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad, CA 92008-1 989
Dear Mr. Ball:
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-A4-074
This letter is a follow-up req)onse to yourrequest on behalf of City of Carlsbad
Planning Director Michael Holzmiller for informal assistance regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Acf).1
QUESTTON
Is it reasonably foreseeable that govemmental decisions on a condominium
conversion project will have a material financial effect on Hofinan Planning Associates,
when the amount payable under its standmd fee agreement is not contingent on the
outcome of those govemmental decisions?
CONCLUSION
The terms of the standard fee agreement, by themselves, are not a sufficient basis
for conclusions regarding the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of governmental
decisions concerning this project.
FACTS
This request for advice is a follow-up to the Ball Advice Letter, No. I-03-286,
concerning the responsibilities of City of Carlsbad Planning Director Michael Holzmiller.
The prior advice letter gave Mr. Holzmiller a framework for appllng the conflict of
interest rules in light of his spouse's employment by a consultant which represents
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. Informal assistance does not confer the immunity
provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
qtqi


FileNo. I-04-074
Page No. 2
developers on development applications before the city. We were able to determine that
Hofman Planning Associates ("Hofman') is a source of income to Mr. Holzrniller, and
therefore we advised that Mr. Holzrniller apply the materiality standards set forth in
regulation 18705-l to determine whether a particular governmental decision would
foreseeably have a material financial effect on Hofinan. Because we did not have any
information on the economic size of HofinarL we were unable to identiff the precise
materiality standard applicable to Hofman, We were likewise unable to determine
whether it was reasonably foreseeable that anyparticulm governmental decision would
have financial effects on Hofman (material orotherwise) because we had no information
regarding any specific govemmental decision.
Mr- Holzrniller now seeks advice on whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a
governmental decision would have a material financial effect on Hofrnan, in light of a
standard fee agreement between Hofrnan and a certain developer who is processing an
application with the City of Carlsbad. Under this fee agreement, Hofrnan will provide
planning services in connection with the project, charged at hourly rates to an rmspecified
total. A "Scope of Work" attachme'nt referencd in the fee agreement was not provided.
The fee agreement does not allow for additional compensation to Hofinan as a result of
any govenrmental decision which may be made on the project. The agreement states that
it contains the entire understanding between the parties. Mr. Holzrniller indicated to you
that this agree,ment is representative of all such agreerrents with developers who have
applications before the city. We have no information regarding this project apmt from
theprovisions of the fee agreement.
Although the fee agreement specifies the client's palm:rent obligations for
"planning se,rvices," the agreement does not describe what is meant bythat term, nor does
it describe the nature or circumstances of any anticipated governmental decision relative
to the project. However, you call our attention to a standmd disclaimer of warranty
providing as follows:
'Additionally, client acknowledges that HOFMAN
PLANNING ASSOCIATES has made no warrant)/ or
guarantee regarding the successfirl outcome of the matters
for which HOFMAN PLANNING ASSOCIATES is
retained, and all expressions relative thereto are matters of
its opinion only."
For purposes of this letter, we assume that some govemme,ntal decisions will
be made at some point regarding the project in question, and we understand that the
warranty disclaimer can be read to mean that Hofrnan's fee is not expressly contingent on
the outcome of any governmental decision. We do not have the information necessary to
determine the size of a financial effect on Hofrnan which would be regarded as
"material," but the thrust of your question does not require that we resolve that particular
point.


File No. I-04-074
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
You ask that we determine the foreseeability of a financial effect on Hofrnan,
attributable to governmental decisions on the project in question. As we noted in our
prior leffer, an effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely''
to occur. (Regulation 18706:- In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC ops. 198.) whetherthe
financial conseque,nces of a governmental decision are "substantially likely'' at the time
the decision is made depends on the specific facts surroundingthe decision. A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand,
if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Id.')
Simply because the fee agreement does not make Hofrnan's palm'rent contingent
on the outcome of governmental decisions, we cannot rule out the possibility that govern-
mental decisions might have foreseeable financial effects on Hofrnan. For example, the
outcome of governmental decisions might increase or decrease the amounts paid under
the fee agreement or such decisions might foreseeably affect firrther contracts - and
income to Hofman - from this client or from others. The terms ofHofinan's standard fee
agreement provide no information on facts or circumstances external to the agreement.
If the Commission does not know all material facts surrounding a specific
govemmental decision, it cannot reach a conclusion on the reasonably foreseeable
financial effects of that decision. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I FPPC Qps 7I.) We suggest tha! armd with
an understanding of the facts and circrmtstances surroundingparticular governrnental
decisions, Mr. Holzmiller can ordinarily determine forhimself whether a material
financial effect on Hofman is "reasonably foreseeable." If he encounters an especially
complex set of circumstances which make the foreseeability determination difficult, we
would be happy to help him order and evaluate the facts. But we must know the material
facts bearing on the question before lve can offer such assistance.
If you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322-56ffi.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Ge,neral CounselL
T,
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
S enior Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
LTwjg

Is it reasonably foreseeable that govemmental decisions on a condominium
conversion project will have a material financial effect on Hofinan Planning Associates,
when the amount payable under its standmd fee agreement is not contingent on the
outcome of those govemmental decisions?

for conclusions regarding the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of governmental
decisions concerning this project.
